input.background: In 1978–79, a random sample of 1007 residents (608 men and 399 women) of the Lothian region was surveyed about their alcohol consumption over the previous seven days. In 1981, due to an increase in taxation and brewers' prices, the cost of alcoholic beverages rose faster than the retail price index for the first time in over 30 years. A follow-up survey was conducted in 1981–82 on 676 'regular drinkers' (those who reported drinking in the first survey). Over the three years, the cost of alcoholic beverages rose by 61%, while the retail price index rose by 52%. Average earnings increased slightly more than the retail price index, but unemployment in the Edinburgh area rose significantly. Of the 676 'regular drinkers,' 463 (69%) were successfully re-interviewed, while 213 were not due to various reasons (e.g., untraceable, left the region, refusal, illness, or death). The authors reported a reduction in alcohol consumption and adverse effects among the 463 re-interviewed subjects and attributed this to the rising cost of alcohol relative to income.
input.question: If the data showed a reduction in alcohol consumption among the 463 reinterviewed subjects, could the authors reasonably conclude that it was due to the rise in excise duty on alcohol?

output.answer: No. First, we cannot necessarily interpret two simultaneous changes over time as being causally related. Second, we would expect them to have found a reduction in the reported alcohol consumption, because the sample was biased to include only those drinking in the first study. If they had re-interviewed only those not drinking in the first study, we would expect them to have found an increase. This is one form of the phenomenon known as 'regression to the mean', and occurs when we remeasure a quantity on a sample selected by a restricted range of the same quantity on a previous occasion. Thus even when there has been no change over time, this study would be expected to have shown a decrease in alcohol consumption.
